content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 6, 2017

Brian S. Harnik, Esq.
Roemer & Harnik LLP
45-025 Manitou Drive
Indian Wells, California 92210

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-122

Dear Mr. Harnik:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Palm Desert Mayor Jan Harnik regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

Because your question is general in nature and not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Mayor from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the City's regulation of marijuana if her husband's law firm represents a marijuana business that has had exploratory discussions with the City about potentially locating a marijuana research laboratory within the City in the future?

CONCLUSION

The Act would prohibit the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the City's regulation of marijuana if the Mayor has a source-of-income interest in the marijuana business and the decision at issue will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on that marijuana business.

___________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-17-122
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the husband of Palm Desert Mayor Jan Harnik and her authorized representative. A
business entity in the marijuana industry recently asked you and your law firm to represent it in
litigation concerning shareholder disputes and corporate governance issues. The marijuana
business's lawsuit has been filed in a forum where your firm regularly appears. For confidentiality
purposes, your firm does not disclose potential clients.

The marijuana business at issue does not currently do business in the City, and has never
done business in the City to the best of your knowledge. However, the marijuana business has had
exploratory discussions with City staff about potentially locating a marijuana research laboratory in
the City in the future. Within the region, the marijuana business has an approved project in the City
of Coachella, where it owns six acres, and a project currently being considered for approval in the
City of Colusa, where it has 11 acres under contract.

The City is in the process of considering marijuana regulations. Mayor Harnik is currently
serving on a City committee that has been conducting public sessions to gather feedback from the
community and interested parties. It is anticipated that the City Council will consider the potential
marijuana regulations within 60 to 90 days.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. Of the
interests identified in Section 87103, decisions relating to the City's potential marijuana regulations
and the facts presented implicate the following:

• Business Interest: An interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or
indirect investment worth $2,000 or more, or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds a position of management. (Section 87103(a) and (d).)

Section 87103's definition of "indirect investment" includes any investment owned by an
official's spouse in which the spouse owns a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103(c).)
Therefore, assuming you have invested $2,000 or more in your law firm and own a 10-percent or
greater interest in the law firm, the Mayor would have a business interest in that law firm.

• Source-of-Income Interest: An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months of the decision.
(Section 87103(c).)

The Act's definition of "income" includes, among other things, a pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns a 10-percent interest
or greater. (Section 82030(a).) The definition of "income" also includes any community property
interest in a spouse's income. (Ibid.) Therefore, assuming you own a 10-percent interest or greater

File No. I-17-122
Page No. 3

in your law firm, and the Mayor's community-property interest in the income from the law firm
aggregates $500 or more in the 12 months preceding the decision, the Mayor would have a source-
of-income interest in the law firm.

However, the Act's definition of "income" does not include income received from "any
source outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, or not having done
business within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any statement or action is required under this title." (Ibid.)

Your facts indicate that the marijuana business at issue has had exploratory discussions with
City staff about potentially locating a research laboratory in the City in the future. Therefore, it is
"planning to do business" within the City. Accordingly, if the Mayor's community-property interest
in your income from the law firm's potential work for the marijuana business aggregates to $500 or
more in the 12 months preceding the decision, the Mayor would also have a source-of-income
interest in that marijuana business.

• Personal Finances: An interest in the official's personal finances or those of an immediate
family member. (See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal
finances.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to
be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision; and an interest is
explicitly involved if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of
any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest.

If the official is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18701(b) provides that
the decision's effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to an official's source-of-
income interest. As noted above, the Mayor would have a source-of-income interest in the
marijuana business at issue if her community-property interest in your income from the law firm's
potential work for the marijuana business aggregates to $500 or more. Regulation 18702.3(a)
provides, in pertinent part, that the decision's effect on a source of income is material if:

"(1) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is
otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding; or

* * *

"(4) The source is a business entity that will be financially affected under the
standards as applied to a financial interest in Regulation 18702.1."

File No. I-17-122
Page No. 4

Regulation 18702.1 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to business interests. Subdivision (a) of that section provides the materiality standards applicable to a business entity that is explicitly involved in a decision. For all other decisions affecting a business entity, subdivision (b) provides that "the financial effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the … value of a privately-held business entity."

Therefore, a decision relating to the City's regulation of marijuana would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's potential source-of-income interest in the marijuana business at issue, if that business is explicitly involved in the decision, or if the decision would contribute to a change in the business's value.³ Thus, the Act would prohibit the Mayor from taking part in those decisions if the Mayor has a source-of-income interest in the marijuana business and the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on that marijuana business.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By:   Matthew F. Christy
      Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

___________________________

³ Because we have determined that decisions relating to the City's regulation of marijuana may have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's potential source-of-income interest in the marijuana business at issue, we do not further analyze those decisions' effect on the Mayor's other financial interests.
